sourceRefs.fallbackText: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a simple data interchange format. Syntactically it resembles the objects and lists of JavaScript. It is most commonly used for communication between web back-ends and JavaScript programs running in the browser, but it is used in many other places, too. Its home page, json.org , provides a wonderfully clear and concise def

sourceRefs.fallbackText: With the json package it’s a snap to read and write JSON data from your Go programs.

sourceRefs.textQuote: Only data structures that can be represented as valid JSON will be encoded:

highSignalBlocks.text: Only data structures that can be represented as valid JSON will be encoded:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: JSON objects only support strings as keys; to encode a Go map type it must be of the form map[string]T (where T is any Go type supported by the json package).
sourceRefs.textQuote: JSON objects only support strings as keys; to encode a Go map type it must be of the form map[string]T (where T is any Go type supported by the json package).

highSignalBlocks.text: JSON objects only support strings as keys; to encode a Go map type it must be of the form map[string]T (where T is any Go type supported by the json package).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Channel, complex, and function types cannot be encoded.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Channel, complex, and function types cannot be encoded.

highSignalBlocks.text: Channel, complex, and function types cannot be encoded.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Cyclic data structures are not supported; they will cause Marshal to go into an infinite loop.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Cyclic data structures are not supported; they will cause Marshal to go into an infinite loop.

highSignalBlocks.text: Cyclic data structures are not supported; they will cause Marshal to go into an infinite loop.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Pointers will be encoded as the values they point to (or ’null’ if the pointer is nil ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Pointers will be encoded as the values they point to (or ’null’ if the pointer is nil ).

highSignalBlocks.text: Pointers will be encoded as the values they point to (or ’null’ if the pointer is nil ).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The json package only accesses the exported fields of struct types (those that begin with an uppercase letter). Therefore only the exported fields of a struct will be present in the JSON output.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The json package only accesses the exported fields of struct types (those that begin with an uppercase letter). Therefore only the exported fields of a struct will be present in the JSON output.

highSignalBlocks.text: The json package only accesses the exported fields of struct types (those that begin with an uppercase letter). Therefore only the exported fields of a struct will be present in the JSON output.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: How does Unmarshal identify the fields in which to store the decoded data? For a given JSON key "Foo" , Unmarshal will look through the destination struct’s fields to find (in order of preference):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unmarshal will decode only the fields that it can find in the destination type. In this case, only the Name field of m will be populated, and the Food field will be ignored. This behavior is particularly useful when you wish to pick only a few specific fields out of a large JSON blob. It also means that any unexported fields in the destination struct will be
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unmarshal will decode only the fields that it can find in the destination type. In this case, only the Name field of m will be populated, and the Food field will be ignored. This behavior is particularly useful when you

highSignalBlocks.text: Unmarshal will decode only the fields that it can find in the destination type. In this case, only the Name field of m will be populated, and the Food field will be ignored. This behavior is particularly useful when you wish to pick only a few specific fields out of a large JSON blob. It also means that any unexported fields in the destination struct will be unaffected by Unmarshal .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unmarshaling that data into a FamilyMember value works as expected, but if we look closely we can see a remarkable thing has happened. With the var statement we allocated a FamilyMember struct, and then provided a pointer to that value to Unmarshal , but at that time the Parents field was a nil slice value. To populate the Parents field, Unmarshal allocated
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unmarshaling that data into a FamilyMember value works as expected, but if we look closely we can see a remarkable thing has happened. With the var statement we allocated a FamilyMember struct, and then provided a pointe

highSignalBlocks.text: Unmarshaling that data into a FamilyMember value works as expected, but if we look closely we can see a remarkable thing has happened. With the var statement we allocated a FamilyMember struct, and then provided a pointer to that value to Unmarshal , but at that time the Parents field was a nil slice value. To populate the Parents field, Unmarshal allocated a new slice behind the scenes. This is typical of how Unmarshal works with the supported reference types (pointers, slices, and maps).

highSignalBlocks.text: and the sending party can populate the Cmd field and/or the Msg field of the top-level JSON object, depending on the type of message they want to communicate. Unmarshal , when decoding the JSON into an IncomingMessage struct, will only allocate the data structures present in the JSON data. To know which messages to process, the programmer need simply test that either Cmd or Msg is not nil .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The json package provides Decoder and Encoder types to support the common operation of reading and writing streams of JSON data. The NewDecoder and NewEncoder functions wrap the io.Reader and io.Writer interface types.

highSignalBlocks.text: func NewDecoder(r io.Reader) *Decoder func NewEncoder(w io.Writer) *Encoder

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here’s an example program that reads a series of JSON objects from standard input, removes all but the Name field from each object, and then writes the objects to standard output:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here’s an example program that reads a series of JSON objects from standard input, removes all but the Name field from each object, and then writes the objects to standard output:

highSignalBlocks.text: Here’s an example program that reads a series of JSON objects from standard input, removes all but the Name field from each object, and then writes the objects to standard output:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: package main import ( "encoding/json" "log" "os" ) func main() { dec := json.NewDecoder(os.Stdin) enc := json.NewEncoder(os.Stdout) for { var v map[string]interface{} if err := dec.Decode(&v); err != nil { log.Println(err) return } for k := range v { if k != "Name" { delete(v, k) } } if err := enc.Encode(&v); err != nil { log.Println(err) } } }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Due to the ubiquity of Readers and Writers, these Encoder and Decoder types can be used in a broad range of scenarios, such as reading and writing to HTTP connections, WebSockets, or files.
